text.content: Participation-based student final performance prediction model through interpretable Genetic Programming: Integrating learning analytics, educational data mining and theory

title.plain_text: Participation-based student final performance prediction model through interpretable Genetic Programming: Integrating learning analytics, educational data mining and theory

tables.heading: Summary Table
rows: [Object, Subject, Tools, Community, Rules, Division of Labor]

On a first level, the logs were categorized when it comes to 4 participation dimensions.

The preprocessed dataset was then classified according to one of the 6 features used.

Target variable was obtained from clustering analysis. Clusters used as input human-evaluated assessments of each student’s achievement.

GP-IRCM
NNge
Random Tree
Logistic Regression
Perceptron
Naive Bayes

Authors present a theory-driven approach that tries to optimize performance and prediction in a CSCL setting.

The work starts with the categorization of learning activities on Geogebra according to participation dimensions. These dimensions help a secondary categorization of the features into the Activity Theory constructs.

These are then used to predict the performance labels that result from clustering. 

Different algorithms are compared and the difference in performance between GP and other candidate algorithms is done using a t-test. Metrics are evaluated as an aggregate (all 5 labels) and specifically for the at-risk label.

GP ICRM outperforms all other options when it comes to overall predictions. Naive Bayes, however, outperforms the remaining models.

Authors follow up with an analysis of the understandability of the models:

Random Tree and GP ICRM are the best models on this mark.

Results show that student participation can be used to predict student performance.

Level of students is not mentioned → different levels of education usually exhibit different behaviors. 

No early prediction.

Alleged ease of use requires extensive pre-classification of learning activities into the activity theory constructs.

Small sample size  and baseline models selected for comparison are all rather “simple”.

Performance labels are inferred by a clustering algorithm.

No assessment of quality of work generated into the features.

Difficult to reproduce if unfamiliar with the VMT environment.

blocks.text: Abstract

text.content: Abstract

rich_text.plain_text: Abstract

blocks.text: Common issues with predictive models of student performance stem from lack of practicality and/or interpretability.  Authors review and propose a different approach to creating predictive models that is, they argue, more usable.

text.content: Common issues with predictive models of student performance stem from lack of practicality and/or interpretability.  Authors review and propose a different approach to creating predictive models that is, they argue, more usable.

rich_text.plain_text: Common issues with predictive models of student performance stem from lack of practicality and/or interpretability.  Authors review and propose a different approach to creating predictive models that is, they argue, more usable.

blocks.text: Authors quantify student participation in the environment as one of 6 different variables. The authors argue that this classification of activities into different variables differentiates them from other approaches as it provides context to the interactions. Then, the authors use Genetic Programming to predict student performance (thus far unspecified). The proposed model outperforms other models in prediction rate and interpretability.

blocks.text: Introduction

text.content: Introduction

rich_text.plain_text: Introduction

blocks.text: Current methods of early detection of students at risk often circle around the notion of detecting at-risk vs not at risk. However, it would be helpful to create models that assist all students → not only those at risk. This would expedite the roles of assessments.

blocks.text: Prior work on performance prediction has usually involved the employment of either Statistical or Data Mining Techniques, but these methods have limitations:

blocks.text: Interpretability is important in early-wrning systems because these are meant to be decision-support systems → meaning that the humans need to trust the model. Also, it will not be helpful for the purposes of telling professors what should be changed in order to improve the prediction. Without these, the authors argue that it would be impossible to explore personalized education.

rich_text.plain_text: Interpretability is important in early-wrning systems because these are meant to be decision-support systems → meaning that the humans need to trust the model. Also, it will not be helpful for the purposes of telling professors what should be changed in order to improve the prediction. Without these, the authors argue that it would be impossible to explore personalized education.

blocks.text: As mentioned, previous work often involves the use of non-interpretable models and a very simple binary flag of at-risk vs not at-risk. This causes concern because these early-warning systems can soon become tools to intervene only on those who fail→punitive interventions. Another common limitation is the lack of theory embedding in the models, both the models used for prediction as the ones used for e-g- feature selection.

blocks.text: This is seen by the authors as a huge flaw, calling for the development of a principled, theory-based method for the synthetizing of factors from factors.

blocks.text:
The authors intend to use a Genetic Algorithm for predictions, arguing that it offers a good tradeoff between predictive power and interpretability. To provide interpretability to actions, authors draw from Hratsinski model of online learning as essencial towards learning. For that, the authors label activities according to 6 different possible aspects. The 6 aspects are then gfiven as inputs to the GA.

The endpoint of the work is a Predictive Model that achieves good performance using 6 different features and that allows educators to understand the causes for a specific prediction.

blocks.text: Data sources for early warning systems  often involve the use student information, learning management systems and/or financial information system. With time, the encoding of this data may change → encoding of learning activities for example.

blocks.text: Most of the models used, such as neural networks or SVM are called black-box models, as they focus on predictive power and not interpretability. There are, however, some studies with white-box models with a much higher emphasis on understanding reasons behind a prediction: for example Bayesian networks. However, these pale in comparison to Decision Tree-based or Rules-based models.

blocks.text: Still, non-parametric models tend to require a lot more data than parametric ones, which is an increased hardship in educational contexts (likely with the exception of MOOCs). GP appears as an elegant alternative that provides interpretability while working reasonably well on smaller datasets. Thus, the authors attempt to use GP to balance multiple constraints:

blocks.text: A framework that links individual and social behavior. According to it, learning is a joint activity of learners, tools and tools. Learning is thus a result of social participation, not mere practice. It involves 6 constructs:

text.content: Methods

rich_text.plain_text: Methods

blocks.text: Analysis of the logs of a math course comprised of 4 modules. In total, the course had 122 students. All activities took place in a Computer-Supported Collaborative Learning environment. The logs from GeoGebra categorizes data in different event types: Awareness, Geogebra, System, Chat and Whiteboard. From these logs, the authors created new measures of participation:

blocks.text: Genetic Programming

text.content: Genetic Programming

rich_text.plain_text: Genetic Programming

blocks.text: GP tests different candidate prediction models, in this case Rules-based models, and assesses their fitness. Then, it iterates over multiple generations in order to minimize the fitness of the candidate solutions. The authors consider the use of 3 genetic operations: cross-over, selection and mutation.

text.content: GP tests different candidate prediction models, in this case Rules-based models, and assesses their fitness. Then, it iterates over multiple generations in order to minimize the fitness of the candidate solutions. The authors consider the use of 3 genetic operations: cross-over, selection and mutation.

blocks.text: The results show that the GP ICRM algorithm outperforms all other options on all metrics, at the level of overall predictions. Moreover, it is only superseded in the identification of students at risk by the Naive Bayes classifier.

blocks.text: The authors followup with a sample showcase of a model built using GP IRCM as it is the model that generated less rules and the simpler rules (even though IMHO the interpretability of these constructs hinges so much on theoretical understanding of the constructs that it sort of looses its mark).
